Why I Recommend Engineers Write a Blog
minimark
h2
intro
Intro
p
A lot of us run into landmines and bugs while learning and developing — and those experiences are gold.
If you don't record them, that's a waste, not to mention the chance you'll hit the same problem again. I really recommend blogging — let me explain why.
img
https://i.imgur.com/tE0jDbV.png
cant-i-just-write-in-my-own-notes-app
"Can't I just write in my own notes app?"
Sure, you can — but typically your notes are written for you and you alone.

That means you might write very casually, glossing over things. Has this ever happened to you: you remember hitting some specific landmine or concept, you check your notes, you find it actually IS in your notes — but you can't understand your own writing? If so, your notes were too casual.
But if you turn it into a blog post, the outcome shifts.
Why? Because your blog is fully public. Any post is visible to anyone.

So when writing, you naturally want it to be clearer and more polished. Because others will see it, you want what you write to be accurate, so you reference other articles. Through that process, you absorb a lot of knowledge without realizing it.
but-what-if-i-write-it-badly-im-afraid-of-misleading-people
"But what if I write it badly? I'm afraid of misleading people"
Nobody writes correctly from day one. You can try writing it first, then look at how others describe and explain the same knowledge. By comparison, you can tell if your own writing is correct.
If you find errors, keep revising until you think it's right. Through that, you keep absorbing knowledge and training your ability to express it.
but-what-if-i-write-badly-and-people-laugh-at-me
"But what if I write badly and people laugh at me?"
Nobody will laugh, even though your post is fully public.
People reading articles — if they find a mistake, the most common behavior is just to move on to the next article. They won't go out of their way to mock you.

And there are plenty of generous experts online willing to share and help. When your post is public, those people are effectively double-checking your understanding for you. That's indirect help — and you're also indirectly helping a lot of others.
what-if-i-write-badly-and-people-call-me-a-loser
"What if I write badly and people call me a loser?"
Nobody writes well from day one — let alone comprehensively.
Same as when we first learn to code — nobody writes beautiful code from day one. We improve through ongoing learning and iteration. A blog post follows the same pattern.
but-others-have-already-written-about-this-do-i-need-to-write-it-again
"But others have already written about this — do I need to write it again?"
Common question. The answer: "Yes, write it."
That's their output of the knowledge, not yours. It's the same idea as only being able to copy-paste — when most of your features are copy-pasted, debugging them is hard.
So if you only consume knowledge but never output it, you don't grow.
Plus, every person describes things from a different angle. Same knowledge point, but one person's framing might click for you, while another's might feel hard to follow.
Writing a post is internalizing the knowledge yourself, which embeds it deeper in your memory.
but-i-dont-know-how-to-start
"But I don't know how to start"

It's always hard to start. Try a small bit first — don't aim for a super long professional post on day one. Start small, accumulate, keep iterating. You'll find your posts grow longer and more complete over time.
but-i-dont-know-what-to-write-about
"But I don't know what to write about"

Try writing about what you're learning in your courses. Even if it seems basic, the moment you try to seriously output it, you'll notice gaps in your understanding. To write that post, you'll dig deep and reference related articles — that process alone gives you a lot to write about.
"Do I have to self-host?"
No absolute answer. I personally use Hexo, but you could pick Medium, WordPress, etc.
ok-but-is-blogging-only-good-for-these-things
"OK, but is blogging only good for these things?"

There's more, including some hidden benefits — job hunting and interviews. Some of my friends got noticed by interviewers because of their blog. Interviewers saw them as someone consistently committed to growth.
It's also brand-building. Imagine you're the interviewer, and the candidate is the author of a blog you regularly read — your confidence in them goes up. Right?
So instead of needing self-introductions in interviews, why not let people get to know you from the start?
These benefits alone are reason enough to start writing. Like this post — it's just a simple learning-experience share, but it might help others, and it lets me look back on my own learning.
I'll leave the remaining benefits for you to discover yourself.
Learning

A lot of us run into landmines and bugs while learning and developing — and those experiences are gold. If you don't record them, that's a waste, not to mention the chance you'll hit the same problem again. I really recommend blogging — let me explain why.
